annotations.label: Gradient (informal)

anchor.snippet: the gradient of a scalar-valued differentiable function

annotations.note: `gradient f x` is the Mathlib definition of the gradient of a scalar-valued differentiable function on a Hilbert space.

annotations.label: Direction of fastest increase

anchor.snippet: the direction of the gradient is the direction in which the function increases most quickly

annotations.note: No `steepest`/`fastest` ascent characterization of the gradient is stated in Mathlib's `Gradient` API.

annotations.label: Stationary point

anchor.snippet: a point where the gradient is the zero vector is known as a stationary point

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `IsCritical`/`StationaryPt`/`CriticalPt` definition for zeros of the gradient.

annotations.label: Gradient via partial derivatives

anchor.snippet: whose components are the partial derivatives

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `gradient` abstractly via Riesz duality; the component/partial-derivative formula is only implicit through tools like `fderiv_pi`, not as a named `gradient` formula.

annotations.label: Non-differentiable function with partial derivatives

anchor.snippet: is not differentiable at the origin as it does not have a well defined tangent plane

annotations.note: This Wikipedia counterexample is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient dual to total derivative

anchor.snippet: The gradient is dual to the total derivative

annotations.note: `inner_gradient_left` proves `⟪∇ f x, y⟫ = fderiv 𝕜 f x y`, the Riesz duality identifying the gradient with the total derivative.

annotations.label: Temperature in a room

anchor.snippet: Consider a room where the temperature is given by a scalar field

annotations.note: This physical motivating example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Height of a surface

anchor.snippet: Consider a surface whose height above sea level

annotations.note: Geographic-height motivating example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Slope along a road

anchor.snippet: Suppose that the steepest slope on a hill is 40%

annotations.note: This numerical slope example is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient gives directional derivative on hill

anchor.snippet: the gradient of H dotted with a unit vector gives the slope of the hill in the direction of the vector

annotations.note: `HasGradientAt.fderiv_apply` gives `fderiv 𝕜 f x y = ⟪f', y⟫`, the general directional-derivative-via-inner-product fact; the unit-vector "slope" framing is not specifically formalized.

annotations.label: Gradient (formal definition)

anchor.snippet: The gradient of f is defined as the unique vector field whose dot product with any vector

annotations.note: `gradient f x := (InnerProductSpace.toDual 𝕜 F).symm (fderiv 𝕜 f x)` defines the gradient by its inner-product duality with the differential.

annotations.label: Coordinate-independence

anchor.snippet: The magnitude and direction of the gradient vector are independent of the particular coordinate representation

annotations.note: Mathlib's gradient is intrinsically defined, so coordinate-invariance is implicit but not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Gradient in Cartesian coordinates

anchor.snippet: In the three-dimensional Cartesian coordinate system with a Euclidean metric

annotations.note: No explicit `gradient` formula in Cartesian/EuclideanSpace coordinates is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient computation in Cartesian

anchor.snippet: For example, the gradient of the function

annotations.note: This worked Cartesian-gradient example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient in cylindrical coordinates

anchor.snippet: In cylindrical coordinates , the gradient is given by

annotations.note: Mathlib has no gradient formula in cylindrical coordinates.

annotations.label: Gradient in spherical coordinates

anchor.snippet: In spherical coordinates with a Euclidean metric, the gradient is given by

annotations.note: Mathlib has no gradient formula in spherical coordinates.

annotations.label: Gradient in general coordinates

anchor.snippet: Using Einstein notation , the gradient can then be written as

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Einstein-notation/metric-tensor gradient formula.

annotations.label: Gradient in orthogonal coordinates

anchor.snippet: If the coordinates are orthogonal we can easily express the gradient

annotations.note: Mathlib has no orthogonal-coordinates gradient formula.

annotations.label: Gradient transpose of total derivative

anchor.snippet: they are transpose ( dual ) to each other

annotations.note: `inner_gradient_left` expresses `⟪∇ f x, y⟫ = fderiv 𝕜 f x y`, i.e. the gradient and total derivative are dual under the inner product.

annotations.label: Total differential / exterior derivative

anchor.snippet: is called the total differential or exterior derivative

annotations.note: `fderiv 𝕜 f x` is Mathlib's Fréchet/total differential of a function between normed spaces.

annotations.label: Gradient via differential formula

anchor.snippet: The gradient is related to the differential by the formula

annotations.note: `HasGradientAt.fderiv_apply` establishes `fderiv 𝕜 f x y = ⟪f', y⟫`, the standard `df(v) = ⟨∇f, v⟩` formula.

annotations.label: Linear approximation via gradient

anchor.snippet: The best linear approximation to a function can be expressed in terms of the gradient

annotations.note: `hasGradientAt_iff_isLittleO` states `f x' = f x + ⟪f', x' - x⟫ + o(x' - x)`, the gradient linear approximation.

annotations.label: Gradient via Fréchet derivative

anchor.snippet: then the differential of f is the Fréchet derivative of f

annotations.note: Mathlib's `gradient` is defined as `(InnerProductSpace.toDual 𝕜 F).symm (fderiv 𝕜 f x)`, exactly Riesz-representing the Fréchet derivative.

annotations.label: Linearity of the gradient

anchor.snippet: The gradient is linear in the sense that

annotations.note: Linearity exists at the Fréchet level (`fderiv_add` etc.), but no `gradient_add`/`gradient_smul` lemmas are recorded in `Mathlib.Analysis.Calculus.Gradient.Basic`.

annotations.label: Product rule for gradient

anchor.snippet: the product rule asserts that the product fg is differentiable

annotations.note: The product rule is formalized for `fderiv` (`fderiv_mul`) but not lifted to a `gradient_mul`/`HasGradientAt.mul` lemma in the Gradient file.

annotations.label: Chain rule (parametric curve)

anchor.snippet: First, suppose that the function g is a parametric curve

annotations.note: The chain rule exists for `fderiv` (`fderiv_comp`) but not stated specifically for `f ∘ γ` in terms of the gradient.

annotations.label: Chain rule (higher dimension)

anchor.snippet: More generally, if instead

annotations.note: Multivariable chain rule is in Mathlib for `fderiv` but not specialized to a gradient formula.

annotations.label: Chain rule (outer scalar function)

anchor.snippet: For the second form of the chain rule

annotations.note: The composition rule is in Mathlib for `fderiv`; no dedicated gradient-of-composition lemma.

annotations.label: Level surface / isosurface

anchor.snippet: A level surface, or isosurface , is the set of all points where some function has a given value

annotations.note: Level sets are expressible via `f ⁻¹' {c}` but Mathlib has no `LevelSet`/`Isosurface` definition.

annotations.label: Gradient orthogonal to level sets

anchor.snippet: in this case the gradient of f is orthogonal to the level sets of f

annotations.note: No theorem in Mathlib relates the gradient to the tangent space of a level set.

annotations.label: Gradient normal to embedded hypersurface

anchor.snippet: any embedded hypersurface in a Riemannian manifold can be cut out by an equation

annotations.note: Mathlib does not record gradient-normal-to-hypersurface results on Riemannian manifolds.

annotations.label: Gradient at singular points of algebraic hypersurface

anchor.snippet: an affine algebraic hypersurface may be defined by an equation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no theorem characterizing singular points of an algebraic hypersurface via the gradient.

annotations.label: Gradient field is conservative

anchor.section: Conservative vector fields and the gradient theorem
anchor.snippet: A (continuous) gradient field is always a conservative vector field

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `ConservativeVectorField` definition or gradient-theorem statement.

annotations.label: Converse: conservative field is a gradient

anchor.section: Conservative vector fields and the gradient theorem
anchor.snippet: Conversely, a (continuous) conservative vector field is always the gradient of a function

annotations.note: The converse of the gradient theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient is steepest ascent direction

anchor.section: Gradient is direction of steepest ascent
anchor.snippet: is also the direction of its steepest ascent

annotations.note: No `steepest`/`fastest` ascent theorem about the gradient is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jacobian matrix

anchor.snippet: Then the Jacobian matrix of f is defined to be an

annotations.note: The Fréchet derivative `fderiv` gives the Jacobian as a continuous linear map, but no explicit matrix-of-partials `jacobianMatrix` definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient of a vector field (rectangular)

anchor.snippet: In rectangular coordinates, the gradient of a vector field

annotations.note: The total derivative of a vector field is `fderiv`; the rectangular-coordinate Jacobian matrix form of the vector-field gradient is not separately defined.

annotations.label: Gradient of a vector field (curvilinear)

anchor.snippet: In curvilinear coordinates, or more generally on a curved manifold

annotations.note: No curvilinear/covariant-derivative formulation of the gradient of a vector field is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient of a vector field (invariant form)

anchor.snippet: Expressed more invariantly, the gradient of a vector field

annotations.note: No invariant/tensorial definition of `∇𝐀` as a (1,1)-tensor field is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gradient on Riemannian manifold

anchor.snippet: For any smooth function f on a Riemannian manifold

annotations.note: Mathlib's Riemannian-manifold development (`Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.Riemannian.*`) does not yet contain a gradient definition for smooth scalar functions.

annotations.label: Gradient via musical isomorphism

anchor.snippet: the gradient of a function is related to its exterior derivative

annotations.note: Mathlib has no musical-isomorphism (`♯`/`♭`) machinery on Riemannian manifolds, so `grad f = (df)^♯` is not formalized.
